title: Remote entropy measurement in coupled quantum dots
authors: Owen Sheekey, Tim Child, Elena Cornick, Saeed Fallahi, Geoffrey C. Gardner, Michael J. Manfra, Eran Sela, Yaakov Kleeorin, Yigal Meir, Silvia Lüscher, Joshua Folk
abstract: Recent experiments have demonstrated that measurements of the entropy change associated with the addition of electrons to semiconductor- and graphene-based quantum dots accurately quantify the spin and orbital degeneracy of the states into which they are added. However, measuring more exotic entropies requires probing the entropy change of an entire system in response to an added particle. Here, we demonstrate that Maxwell relation-based measurements probe not only the entropy change associated with the added electron but also that of the surrounding system as it responds to that electron. Using a pair of capacitively coupled GaAs quantum dots, we show that charge measurements on one dot reveal entropy changes associated with the entire two-dot system, both at weak dot--reservoir coupling where microstate counting applies and at stronger coupling where numerical renormalization group calculations are required.
summary: The researchers demonstrated a method to measure the entropy of an entire coupled system by monitoring the charge of a single quantum dot. By applying Maxwell relations to temperature-dependent charge measurements, they successfully probed entropy changes in both weakly and strongly coupled regimes. This establishes quantum dots as effective sensors for the thermodynamic properties of nearby mesoscopic circuits.
main_problem: Developing a method to measure 'remote' entropy—the entropy change of an entire system beyond the local site of an added particle—and testing the ability of a quantum dot to act as an auxiliary sensor for the entropy of a separate, coupled part of a circuit.
main_result: Demonstrated that charge measurements on one quantum dot can reveal entropy changes of an entire coupled two-dot system, validating Maxwell relation-based measurements for detecting both microstate-counting and hybridized regimes.
method: Used Maxwell relations to connect entropy change to the temperature dependence of particle number, implemented via rapid reservoir temperature modulation (Joule heating) and charge sensing via a quantum point contact.
model_or_system: A pair of capacitively coupled GaAs/AlGaAs lateral quantum dots (QD1 and QD2) tunnel-coupled to a shared reservoir, where QD1 is monitored by an adjacent quantum point contact (QPC) charge sensor.
key_observables: Change in QPC current (delta I_CS), change in dot occupation (N), and extracted entropy change (delta S_sys).
important_parameters: Interdot electrostatic interaction (U12), dot-reservoir coupling (Gamma), temperature (T), and gate voltages.
main_assumptions: The model assumes no direct interdot tunneling and relies on the validity of Maxwell relations in the mesoscopic regime; the analysis also assumes the ability to isolate spurious signals from the sensor-dot coupling.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device SEM, charging diagrams, and charge sensor signal maps; Fig 3 shows entropy change measurements across transitions and their evolution with coupling; Fig 4 shows entropy change across the (0,1) to (1,0) transition.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the remote sensing problem, describes the experimental GaAs quantum dot platform, details the thermodynamic measurement protocol using Maxwell relations, presents results in the weak-coupling (microstate counting) and strong-coupling (hybridized) regimes, and discusses signal purification techniques.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates a way to use a local probe to sense non-local thermodynamic properties, which is highly relevant for monitoring entanglement entropy or topological signatures in open quantum systems and many-body circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) A false colour scanning electron micrograph of the device, showing QD1 and QD2 independently tunnel coupled to a thermal reservoir, with a quantum point contact (QPC) to monitor the charge on the dots. b) The charging diagram of the double-dot system, with bright lines representing QD1 charge transitions and faint lines representing QD2 charge transitions (visible through weaker coupling of QD2 to the charge sensor). c) Adjusted charge sensor signal, I′ CS, across the range indicated by the yellow square in (b), but collected using the virtual gate ˜VD instead of VP 1. I′ CS is calculated from ICS by removing a slope and offset, see SI. (N1, N2) pairs denote regions of fixed...

FIG. 3. a) Peak (black) and final (red) measurements of entropy change across the QD1 0 →1 transition, obtained by integrating vertical linecuts from Fig. 2 after scaling to convert ∆ICS to ∂N1/∂T. Solid lines are calculated entropy changes in the weak coupling limit. Shading represents the systematic uncertainty associated with imperfect knowledge of ∆T between hot and cold data. b) Net entropy change for the QD1 0 →1 transition as couplings Γ1,2 to the reservoir are increased. Data match the NRG calculations well using Γ1,2 extracted from independent measurements (see S.I), with only the VP 2 lever arm and a horizontal offset as fitting pa- rameters. The weakly-coupled dataset is the same...

FIG. 2. a) Response of ICS, reflecting N1, to temperature change in the reservoir from 52 to 73 mK, collected at the same time as Fig. 1c. Regions of different QD occupations are marked, with expected spin-resolved microstates listed for each. Vertical linecuts represent trajectories in gate-space varying only ϵ1. Data along these linecuts, b), can be inte- grated following Eq. 1, to yield ∆S, shown in c). Horizontal dotted lines represent the expected ∆S corresponding to the microstates labelled in panel a).

FIG. 4. a,b) Maps of dN/dT analogous to Fig. 2a, for the strongest-coupled setting in Fig. 3, at T = 35, 115 mK for panels a, b respectively. c) Measured ∆S( ˜VD) across the (0,1)-(1,0) transition along the linecuts marked in a,b) cho- sen to be midway between the triple points, as well as an equivalent dataset at 52 mK, showing the collapse in entropy associated with the onset of interdot correlations via the reser- voir.
